agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a courier marketplace order flow spanning 4 services (Order, Inventory, Payment, Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and triggers compensating transactions on failure. Each service exposes an execute and a compensate endpoint. The orchestrator maintains saga state in a durable store, enabling recovery from orchestrator crashes and providing clear observability into in-flight sagas.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga step-by-step: executes forward actions, detects failures, triggers compensating transactions in reverse order, and persists saga state for crash recovery.","boundaries":"Owns saga state machine and step sequencing. Does NOT own any business domain data. Communicates with services via async commands/replies."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (PENDING), confirms orders (CONFIRMED), and cancels orders (CANCELLED) as a compensating action.","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate. Exposes CreateOrder and CancelOrder commands."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves inventory for order line items and releases reservations as a compensating action.","boundaries":"Owns inventory stock and reservation state. Exposes ReserveInventory and ReleaseInventory commands."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges the customer for the order total and issues refunds as a compensating action.","boundaries":"Owns payment transaction records. Exposes ChargePayment and RefundPayment commands."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order and unassigns them as a compensating action.","boundaries":"Owns courier availability and assignment state. Exposes AssignCourier and UnassignCourier commands."},{"name":"Saga State Store","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instances—current step, status, compensation log, and payload. Enables crash recovery and idempotent retries.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. Could be a dedicated table in the orchestrator's database or a shared durable store (e.g., PostgreSQL, DynamoDB)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"Command: CreateOrder {orderId, items[], customerId} → Reply: OrderCreated | OrderCreationFailed. Compensate: CancelOrder {orderId} → Reply: OrderCancelled.","coupling_type":"async command/reply via message broker"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"Command: ReserveInventory {orderId, items[]} → Reply: InventoryReserved | InsufficientStock. Compensate: ReleaseInventory {orderId} → Reply: InventoryReleased.","coupling_type":"async command/reply via message broker"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Command: ChargePayment {orderId, customerId, amount} → Reply: PaymentCharged | PaymentFailed. Compensate: RefundPayment {orderId, transactionId} → Reply: PaymentRefunded.","coupling_type":"async command/reply via message broker"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"Command: AssignCourier {orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation} → Reply: CourierAssigned | NoCourierAvailable. Compensate: UnassignCourier {orderId, courierId} → Reply: CourierUnassigned.","coupling_type":"async command/reply via message broker"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits PlaceOrder request → Saga Orchestrator creates saga instance (state: STARTED). 2. Orchestrator sends CreateOrder → Order Service replies OrderCreated (state: ORDER_CREATED). 3. Orchestrator sends ReserveInventory → Inventory Service replies InventoryReserved (state: INVENTORY_RESERVED). 4. Orchestrator sends ChargePayment → Payment Service replies PaymentCharged (state: PAYMENT_CHARGED). 5. Orchestrator sends AssignCourier → Courier Service replies CourierAssigned (state: COMPLETED). On failure at any step, orchestrator transitions to COMPENSATING and walks backward through completed steps, issuing compensating commands. Final state: COMPENSATED (all rollbacks done) or COMPENSATION_FAILED (manual intervention needed).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"Central coordinator provides explicit control flow, clear saga state visibility, easier debugging and monitoring compared to choreography. With 4 sequential steps and ordered compensation, orchestration avoids the implicit coupling and event-chain complexity of choreography."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transactions","rationale":"Distributed transactions (2PC) are impractical across independent services. Each service provides a semantic undo operation, achieving eventual consistency without distributed locks."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Network failures and retries are inevitable. Every command (forward and compensating) must be idempotent using an idempotency key (sagaId + stepId) to safely retry without side effects."},{"pattern":"State Machine","rationale":"Saga lifecycle is modeled as a finite state machine with well-defined transitions. Prevents invalid state transitions and makes the saga's current position unambiguous after a crash."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern","rationale":"Ensures atomicity between saga state persistence and command publishing. The orchestrator writes the command to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state update, and a relay publishes it to the broker."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED → CANCELLED)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["StockReservation (root: reservationId, linked to orderId, states: RESERVED → RELEASED)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: transactionId, linked to orderId, states: CHARGED → REFUNDED)"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment (root: assignmentId, linked to orderId + courierId, states: ASSIGNED → UNASSIGNED)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: sagaId, tracks currentStep, status, completedSteps[], compensationLog[])"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (Orchestrator dictates commands, Order Service conforms to command schema)"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Explicit control flow, centralized saga state for observability and debugging, straightforward compensation ordering, easier to reason about failure scenarios","sacrifices":"Single coordination point (though stateless + durable store mitigates this), orchestrator becomes a coupling point that must know about all participants","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and listens for others. Avoids central coordinator but creates implicit coupling, makes compensation ordering harder to guarantee, and is difficult to debug with 4+ steps."]},{"decision":"Async command/reply over synchronous HTTP calls","gains":"Temporal decoupling—services don't need to be simultaneously available. Natural retry semantics via message broker. Backpressure handling.","sacrifices":"Higher infrastructure complexity (requires message broker). Slightly higher latency for the happy path. Harder local development setup.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST calls with circuit breakers: simpler infrastructure but tightly couples availability and makes compensation during partial failures more error-prone."]},{"decision":"Outbox pattern for saga state + command atomicity","gains":"Guarantees that state updates and command publications are atomic without distributed transactions. Prevents ghost commands (published but state not saved) and lost commands (state saved but not published).","sacrifices":"Requires a polling relay or CDC mechanism. Adds operational complexity.","alternatives_considered":["Relying on broker acknowledgment after DB commit: leaves a window where DB commits but publish fails, causing stuck sagas."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a sequential list of steps. Adding a step (e.g., a Notification Service after courier assignment) means adding one entry to the step list with its forward and compensating command. No changes to existing services."},{"area":"Retry policies per step","evolution_path":"Each step in the saga definition can have independent retry count, backoff strategy, and timeout. The orchestrator applies these per-step without affecting others."},{"area":"Parallel step execution","evolution_path":"If future requirements allow steps 2 and 3 (inventory + payment) to run in parallel, the state machine can be extended to support fork/join semantics. Compensation would then apply to all branches."},{"area":"Saga variants","evolution_path":"Different order types (express, scheduled, bulk) can define different saga step sequences reusing the same orchestrator engine with different step configurations."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation ordering must be strictly reverse","reason":"Business invariant: you cannot refund before releasing inventory or cancel an order that still has a courier assigned. The reverse-order compensation is a hard constraint, not a design preference."},{"area":"Idempotency contract on all services","reason":"Every participating service MUST implement idempotent command handlers. This is non-negotiable for correctness under retries and crash recovery. Violating this causes duplicate charges, double reservations, etc."},{"area":"Saga state must be durable","reason":"If saga state is lost mid-flight, the system enters an inconsistent state with no recovery path. In-memory-only saga state is unacceptable for a financial flow."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure: a compensating transaction itself fails (e.g., refund API is down)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement retry with exponential backoff for compensating actions. After max retries, transition saga to COMPENSATION_FAILED state and alert operations for manual intervention. Maintain a dead-letter queue for failed compensations."},{"risk":"Orchestrator single point of failure","severity":"high","mitigation":"Make orchestrator stateless (state in durable store). Run multiple instances with message-broker-based partitioning (e.g., Kafka consumer group). On crash, another instance picks up in-flight sagas from the state store."},{"risk":"Stuck sagas due to missing replies","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement per-step timeouts. If no reply within timeout, orchestrator retries (up to limit) then triggers compensation. A background sweeper detects sagas stuck in non-terminal states beyond a threshold and either retries or compensates."},{"risk":"Non-idempotent service implementation causing duplicate side effects","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Enforce idempotency keys in the command contract. Services must check idempotency key before processing. Include idempotency verification in integration tests and contract tests."},{"risk":"Message ordering issues causing out-of-sequence processing","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition messages by sagaId to guarantee ordering per saga. The state machine rejects commands that don't match the expected current step."},{"risk":"Saga state store becomes a bottleneck under high order volume","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Saga state is partitioned by sagaId (natural sharding key). Use a database that supports horizontal partitioning. Saga instances are short-lived and can be archived after completion."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Define the saga as a declarative step list: [{name, forwardCommand, compensatingCommand, timeout, retries}]. The orchestrator engine is generic—it takes any step list and drives it. Each service implements a command handler and a compensation handler, both idempotent. Use a message broker (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka) for command/reply channels. Persist saga state in PostgreSQL with an outbox table. A saga state machine class manages transitions: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → INVENTORY_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COMPLETED, with a parallel COMPENSATING track that walks backward.","files_to_create_or_modify":["saga/OrderSagaDefinition — declarative step list for the order flow","saga/SagaOrchestrator — generic engine: execute steps, handle replies, trigger compensation","saga/SagaStateMachine — state transitions and validation","saga/SagaStateRepository — persistence of saga instances","saga/OutboxRelay — polls outbox table and publishes to broker","order-service/handlers/CreateOrderHandler, CancelOrderHandler","inventory-service/handlers/ReserveInventoryHandler, ReleaseInventoryHandler","payment-service/handlers/ChargePaymentHandler, RefundPaymentHandler","courier-service/handlers/AssignCourierHandler, UnassignCourierHandler","shared/contracts/SagaCommands — command and reply message schemas","shared/contracts/IdempotencyKey — idempotency key generation and validation"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each handler: 1) Check idempotency key → if already processed, return cached reply. 2) Execute business logic within a local transaction. 3) Return reply. Compensation handlers follow the same pattern. The orchestrator: 1) Load saga state. 2) Determine next action. 3) Write state + outbox command in single transaction. 4) Outbox relay publishes command. 5) On reply, repeat from step 1.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use distributed transactions (2PC) — they don't scale and most message brokers don't support them","Do NOT rely on in-memory saga state — crashes will leave orphaned partial transactions","Do NOT skip idempotency — retries WILL happen and duplicate processing corrupts data","Do NOT compensate in forward order — always reverse to maintain business invariants","Do NOT make the orchestrator stateful (in-memory only) — it must be recoverable from the durable store","Do NOT fire-and-forget compensating actions — track their completion and handle failures","Do NOT couple services directly to each other — all coordination flows through the orchestrator"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration saga is the correct pattern for 4 sequential steps with ordered compensation","Compensating transaction design and reverse-order execution","Idempotency and outbox pattern requirements","State machine modeling for saga lifecycle"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific message broker choice depends on existing infrastructure (Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS)","Exact timeout and retry values require load testing and SLA analysis","Whether inventory + payment could safely run in parallel depends on deeper business rule analysis"],"assumptions":["Services are independently deployable and do not share databases","A message broker is available or can be introduced","Each service can implement idempotent handlers","Order volumes justify the infrastructure overhead of an async saga over simpler synchronous compensation"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a courier marketplace order flow spanning 4 services (Order, Inventory, Payment, Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and triggers compensating transactions on failure. Each service exposes an execute and a compensate endpoint. The orchestrator maintains saga state in a durable store, enabling recovery from orchestrator crashes and providing clear observability into in-flight sagas.","architecture":{"components":[{"name"
